title: Thuringian Higher Education Act
text:
HEADNOTE:
1. In organising universities, the legislator must guarantee a sufficient level of participation for holders of the fundamental right of academic freedom and create an overall structure for academic freedom in which decision-making powers and participation rights, influence, information and scrutiny are designed in such a way as to avoid structural risks to the freedom of academic teaching and research. With the present higher education structures, this concerns the relationship between representative bodies and leadership bodies and their appropriate internal design. 2. An appropriate level of participation in representative bodies of holders of the fundamental right of academic freedom requires that they can contribute their expertise as members of the body in question to realise academic freedom and that they can avert risks to academic freedom that may arise from decisions that are inadequate from an academic point of view. 3. The protection from inadequate decisions requires that the overall composition of the bodies that serve to give effect to the right of holders of the fundamental right of academic freedom to participate in the organisation of the university does not allow for considerable non-academic influence in academically relevant decisions. a) When academically relevant decisions are at issue, academic freedom under the first sentence of Article 5.3 of the Basic Law therefore requires that the voting weight of groups whose activities are not directly related to research and teaching is sufficiently limited or that the representatives of such groups have a sufficient link to academia. b) This does not completely rule out the participation of the group of non-academic staff in such decision-making, but it requires that, if they are granted undifferentiated participation rights within the group, they make up a significantly lesser share of votes than the groups that have direct links to research and teaching.

SUMMARY:
I. Professors from several higher education institutions in the Land Thuringia lodged a constitutional complaint challenging provisions of the Thuringian Higher Education Act (hereinafter, the “Act”). The challenged provisions concern the organisation and structure of universities at the central level and decentralised bodies below this level. These provisions are part of an amendment to the law concerning, inter alia, the representation of different groups belonging to the university in self-government, changes to the distribution of powers within the organisation of universities and measures to improve teaching. They provide for two different compositions for the university senate and the decentralised self-government bodies administering the university. The type of matter to be decided determines the composition in which the bodies meet. In the standard composition, the four groups (professors, students, research assistants and employees in facilities management and the administration) each have one quarter of the seats and votes. By contrast, for matters that directly concern teaching (with the exception of its evaluation), research, artistic development projects and the appointment of professors, the group of professors has a narrow majority of seats and votes. The applicants assert that these provisions violate their academic freedom following from the first sentence of Article 5.3 of the Basic Law II. The constitutional complaint is unsuccessful for the most part. It is in large part inadmissible. Most of the challenges that are admissible are unfounded. The challenged provisions grant the applicants a level of participation that gives sufficient consideration to their academic freedom. The first sentence of Article 5.3 of the Basic Law grants individuals a fundamental right to academic freedom and contains a fundamental precept of values that governs the relationship of academia, research and teaching to the state. In organising universities, the legislator must guarantee a sufficient level of participation for holders of the fundamental right of academic freedom and create an overall structure for academic freedom in which decision-making powers and participation rights, influence, information and scrutiny are designed in such a way as to avoid structural risks to the freedom of academic teaching and research. With regard to the internal design of representative bodies of universities, an appropriate level of participation of holders of the fundamental right of academic freedom requires that they can contribute their expertise as members of the body in question to realise academic freedom and that they can avert risks to academic freedom that may arise from decisions that are inadequate from an academic point of view. If professors can prevail over the other groups in academically relevant matters, an appropriate level of participation is ensured. Moreover, the protection from inadequate decisions requires that the overall composition of the bodies that serve to give effect to the right of holders of the fundamental right of academic freedom to participate in the organisation of the university does not allow for considerable non-academic influence in academically relevant decisions. Thus, when academically relevant decisions are at issue, academic freedom under the first sentence of Article 5.3 of the Basic Law requires that the voting weight of groups whose activities are not directly related to research and teaching is sufficiently limited or that the representatives of such groups have a sufficient link to academia. This does not completely rule out the participation of the group of non-academic staff in such decision-making, but it requires that they make up a significantly lesser share of votes than the groups that have direct links to research and teaching. The first sentence of § 35.3, § 35.4 and the first and second sentence of § 40.1 of the Act, which concern the voting rights of representatives of the group of employees in facilities management and the administration for decisions of the university senate (the central self-government body for the university) and other bodies of self-government, are incompatible with academic freedom. The group of employees in facilities management and the administration has the same voting weight in the senate and in decentralised self-government bodies as the group of research assistants and the group of students. The legislator failed to provide for safeguards required by academic freedom against the structural risk of inadequate influence on decisions of the university senate and decentralised self-government bodies. The Federal Constitutional Court declared the provisions to be incompatible with the Basic Law. The provisions will continue to apply until the legislator has enacted new provisions, or 31 March 2027 at the latest.

ADDITIONAL INFORMATION:
Languages: German; English (press release available).

CROSS-REFERENCES:
Federal Constitutional Court: - 1 BvR 3217/07, 24.06.2014, Entscheidungen des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE (Official Journal) 136, 338, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2014:rs20140624.1bvr321707, [GER-2014-2-021], decision available in English on the Court’s website; - 1 BvR 748/06, 20.07.2010, BVerfGE 127, 87, ECLI:DE:BVerfG:2010:rs20100720.1bvr074806, [GER-2010-3-013]; - 1 BvR 424/71, 1 BvR 325/72, 29.05.1973, BVerfGE 35, 79.
